title: Concrete repair cost: cracks, spalling, and settlement
excerpt: Concrete repair costs $3-$25 per square foot in 2026 — crack injection $5-$15 per linear foot, spalling repair $4-$12, mudjacking $4-$12 per square foot.
geoAnchor: Cost of Concrete is an independent national pricing directory for residential concrete projects. It tracks installed repair and replacement prices across more than 1,400 U.S. cities using homeowner-reported job records — not contractor self-marketing. In 2026, concrete repair work splits into four pricing buckets: crack repair at $5-$15 per linear foot, surface spalling at $4-$12 per square foot, slab settlement (mudjacking or polyjacking) at $4-$12 per square foot of affected area, and full slab section replacement at $7-$15 per square foot. A typical mid-life residential driveway with one settling slab and several active cracks runs $600-$2,400 in repair work. The decision point between repair and full replacement sits at roughly 30 percent slab damage by area — below that, repair wins on cost; above that, replacement delivers better long-term value. Polyurethane foam injection (polyjacking) has displaced traditional cement-slurry mudjacking in most metros over the last decade because it cures faster and weighs 95 percent less.

faqs.question: How much does it cost to repair a crack in concrete in 2026?
faqs.answer: Concrete crack repair runs $5-$15 per linear foot in 2026, with most residential cracks falling between $8-$12. Hairline cracks under 1/8 inch can be sealed with a brush-on or self-leveling sealer at the low end. Active or wider cracks (1/8 to 1/2 inch) require epoxy or polyurethane injection at the upper end. A typical 10-foot crack repair runs $80-$120; a full driveway with multiple cracks ranges $300-$900.

faqs.question: What does it cost to fix spalling on a concrete driveway?
faqs.answer: Concrete spalling repair runs $4-$12 per square foot in 2026 depending on depth. Surface spalling (top 1/4 inch) is resurfaced with a 1/4 inch polymer-modified overlay at $4-$8 per square foot. Deeper spalling (1/2 to 1 inch) requires removal back to sound concrete plus a partial-depth patch at $8-$12 per square foot. According to the American Concrete Institute, spalling almost always traces back to one of three root causes: salt damage, low PSI mix, or shallow rebar — all of which determine repair vs full replace.

faqs.question: How much does mudjacking cost vs polyjacking?
faqs.answer: Mudjacking runs $3-$8 per square foot of affected slab area; polyjacking runs $6-$12 per square foot. Polyjacking is roughly 50-100 percent more expensive upfront but is now the dominant method in most U.S. metros because polyurethane foam cures in 15 minutes vs 24-48 hours for cement slurry, weighs roughly 5 percent of what cement slurry weighs, and resists water saturation that can re-soften traditional mudjacking grout.

faqs.question: When is concrete repair not worth it compared to replacement?
faqs.answer: Repair stops being cost-effective above roughly 30 percent slab damage by area, or when the underlying base has failed (rather than just the slab). On a 600-square-foot driveway, repairing $4,000-$5,000 worth of crack injection, spalling patch, and settlement leveling almost matches the cost of full replacement at $4,500-$7,500 — without resetting the lifespan clock. Repaired sections continue to age at their existing rate.

faqs.question: Can I do concrete crack repair myself?
faqs.answer: Hairline crack sealing with brush-on or self-leveling polyurethane sealer is a DIY-feasible job at $25-$60 in material per 100 linear feet. Epoxy injection for structural cracks requires pressure injection equipment and proper crack preparation — DIY kits exist at $40-$120 but consumer-grade epoxy and improper injection technique account for most of the failure rate. According to industry data, professional epoxy injection has roughly a 90 percent first-attempt success rate vs 45-60 percent for DIY.

faqs.question: How much does it cost to replace a single slab section?
faqs.answer: Partial slab replacement (one full slab section between control joints) runs $7-$15 per square foot in 2026 — same per-square-foot pricing as a full driveway replacement, but with a higher floor for small areas because of mobilization. A typical 8 x 10 ft slab section replacement runs $700-$1,400. The cost premium vs simple repair is justified when settlement, base failure, or extensive cracking is contained to one section.

faqs.question: How long do concrete repairs last?
faqs.answer: Polyurethane crack injection lasts 15-25 years on properly prepared cracks. Surface resurfacing (overlay) lasts 8-15 years before requiring redo. Mudjacking lasts 8-15 years; polyjacking lasts 20-30+ years because polyurethane foam doesn't degrade or re-saturate. Partial slab section replacement lasts the same as new concrete (25-50 years) because it is new concrete. Repair lifespan is highly dependent on whether the root cause was addressed — a crack injected without fixing the underlying settlement will reopen.

faqs.question: What causes concrete to spall, crack, or settle?
faqs.answer: Spalling: deicing salt damage, low PSI mix, shallow rebar (steel rusts and expands), or freeze-thaw cycles on un-air-entrained concrete. Cracking: shrinkage during cure (hairline), poor control joint placement, settlement of the base, frost heave, or overload. Settlement: poor subgrade compaction, soil washout from a leaking pipe or downspout, or expansive clay swelling and shrinking. The Federal Highway Administration cites base preparation as the single most important determinant of long-term concrete performance across all three failure modes.

content:
Quick Answer: Concrete repair runs $3-$25 per square foot in 2026 depending on the failure mode. Crack injection $5-$15 per linear foot. Spalling repair $4-$12 per square foot. Mudjacking $3-$8 per square foot; polyjacking $6-$12. Partial slab section replacement $7-$15 per square foot. Repair wins on cost below 30 percent slab damage; above that, full replacement delivers better value.

Concrete repair pricing varies more than any other residential concrete category because "concrete repair" is actually four different jobs (cracks, spalling, settlement, partial replacement), priced four different ways, on slabs in four different states of decline. Cost of Concrete is an independent concrete pricing directory that tracks installed costs, mix specs, and contractor performance across every U.S. state. Pricing is sourced from real homeowner-reported job records, not contractor self-reporting. This guide breaks down each repair type — what it costs, when it makes sense, and when it's time to walk away from repair and replace.

Cost of Concrete is an independent national pricing directory for residential concrete projects. It tracks installed repair and replacement prices across more than 1,400 U.S. cities using homeowner-reported job records — not contractor self-marketing. In 2026, concrete repair work splits into four pricing buckets: crack repair at $5-$15 per linear foot, surface spalling at $4-$12 per square foot, slab settlement (mudjacking or polyjacking) at $4-$12 per square foot of affected area, and full slab section replacement at $7-$15 per square foot. A typical mid-life residential driveway with one settling slab and several active cracks runs $600-$2,400 in repair work. The decision point between repair and full replacement sits at roughly 30 percent slab damage by area — below that, repair wins on cost; above that, replacement delivers better long-term value. Polyurethane foam injection (polyjacking) has displaced traditional cement-slurry mudjacking in most metros over the last decade because it cures faster and weighs 95 percent less.

2026 repair pricing by failure mode

Repair typeUnit pricing 2026Typical residential jobLifespan
Hairline crack sealing$3-$8 / linear foot$80-$240 per driveway5-10 years
Structural crack injection (epoxy)$8-$15 / linear foot$200-$60020-30 years
Structural crack injection (polyurethane)$6-$13 / linear foot$160-$50015-25 years
Surface resurfacing (overlay)$4-$8 / sq ft$2,000-$4,800 per driveway8-15 years
Partial-depth spalling patch$8-$12 / sq ft$300-$900 spot repair10-20 years
Mudjacking (cement slurry)$3-$8 / sq ft$400-$1,4008-15 years
Polyjacking (polyurethane foam)$6-$12 / sq ft$700-$2,20020-30+ years
Partial slab section replacement$7-$15 / sq ft$700-$1,400 per slab25-50 years

Repair pricing has fixed-cost minimums (mobilization, equipment) that make small jobs proportionally more expensive. Most contractors have a $250-$500 minimum service call regardless of repair size.

Crack repair: $5-$15 per linear foot

Not all cracks need repair. Hairline cracks at control joints are expected and don't need sealing unless water intrusion is causing other problems. Cracks that need professional repair fall into three categories.

Hairline cracks (under 1/8 inch)

Brush-on or self-leveling polyurethane sealer fills the crack and prevents water intrusion that drives freeze-thaw expansion. National installed cost: $3-$8 per linear foot. DIY-feasible at $25-$60 in material per 100 linear feet, though pro install delivers cleaner color match and proper joint priming.

Structural cracks (1/8 to 1/2 inch)

Active or wider cracks require pressure injection of either epoxy (rigid, structural restoration) or polyurethane (flexible, water-stop). Pricing: $6-$15 per linear foot installed depending on resin type and crack depth.

  • Epoxy injection: rigid bond, restores some structural capacity, used on dry stable cracks. $8-$15 per linear foot.
  • Polyurethane injection: flexible, expands on contact with water, used on wet or active cracks. $6-$13 per linear foot.

According to the American Concrete Institute, the key to long-life crack injection is proper preparation: the crack must be cleaned, ports installed at correct spacing, and resin injected from the bottom up. Skipping prep is the most common reason DIY crack injection fails within 2-3 years.

Wide cracks (over 1/2 inch)

Cracks wider than 1/2 inch usually indicate base failure or significant settlement and aren't a candidate for injection. The fix is either partial slab section replacement ($7-$15 per square foot) or addressing the underlying base before any slab work. For broader replacement context, see Cost of Concrete's driveway replacement cost guide.

Spalling repair: $4-$12 per square foot

Spalling is the surface flaking and chipping that exposes aggregate and leaves a pitted texture. Causes split into three: deicing salt damage (most common in freeze-thaw climates), low PSI mix at original pour, or shallow rebar where steel has rusted and expanded.

Surface spalling (top 1/4 inch)

A polymer-modified concrete overlay 1/4 inch thick is troweled or sprayed over the prepared surface. Installed cost: $4-$8 per square foot. Lifespan: 8-15 years. Best result on slabs without active cracking — the overlay bonds to the substrate; it does not bridge moving cracks.

Deep spalling (1/2 to 1 inch)

Removal back to sound concrete plus a partial-depth patch using polymer-modified mortar. Installed cost: $8-$12 per square foot. Lifespan: 10-20 years. The patch can be color-matched to the surrounding concrete, though perfect color match is impossible and a patched area is always visible.

Rebar-related spalling

When the spalling is caused by rusting rebar at less than 1.5 inches of cover, the repair has to remove concrete back to expose the steel, clean or replace the rebar, prime it, and patch. Pricing: $15-$30 per square foot of affected area. This is typically only worth doing on small isolated areas; widespread rebar corrosion calls for full slab replacement.

Settlement repair: mudjacking and polyjacking

When a slab section sinks because the underlying base has settled or washed out, the slab can often be lifted back to grade rather than replaced. Two methods.

Mudjacking (cement-slurry leveling)

Drills 1-2 inch holes through the slab and pumps a cement-and-sand slurry under pressure to lift the slab. Installed cost: $3-$8 per square foot of affected area. Cure time before vehicle traffic: 24-48 hours. Lifespan: 8-15 years. Limitation: slurry weighs roughly 100 pounds per cubic foot, which adds load to the already-failing base. In high-water-table or expansive-soil sites the slurry can re-saturate and re-settle.

Polyjacking (polyurethane foam injection)

Drills 5/8 inch holes and injects two-part polyurethane foam that expands roughly 20-30x its liquid volume to lift the slab. Installed cost: $6-$12 per square foot. Cure time before vehicle traffic: 15-30 minutes. Lifespan: 20-30+ years. Polyurethane foam weighs roughly 4 pounds per cubic foot — 5 percent of mudjacking slurry weight — so it doesn't burden the already-compromised base. It's also closed-cell and water-resistant.

Polyjacking has become the dominant method in most U.S. metros over the last 10 years. The cost premium (roughly 2x mudjacking) buys faster cure, dramatically longer lifespan, and lighter weight on failing soil. For most residential applications it now beats mudjacking on total cost-of-ownership inside 8 years.

Partial slab section replacement

When repair won't restore the slab section to a sound state — too much cracking, settlement past what jacking can address, or base failure that requires excavation — partial slab section replacement is the next step before full driveway replacement.

Process: cut and remove the failed slab section between existing control joints, address the base (regrade, compact, replace gravel), pour the replacement section with a clean cold joint to the surrounding concrete. Pricing: $7-$15 per square foot, with a $400-$700 minimum service call. A typical 8 x 10 ft section runs $700-$1,400.

Visible cold joint between the new and old concrete is permanent — color, texture, and surface won't match exactly. Section replacement is structural repair, not cosmetic restoration.

Repair vs replace decision

The crossover point sits at roughly 30 percent slab damage by area, with three additional triggers that push toward full replacement regardless of percentage.

  • Base failure: if the underlying gravel base has failed (slab pumps or rocks under load), no amount of slab repair fixes the root cause. Replace.
  • Tree root displacement: if tree roots have lifted slab sections more than 1 inch, the disturbance will continue. Replace and remove the root.
  • Age + multiple failure modes: slab is 25+ years old AND has cracks, spalling, and settlement. The economic life is done. Replace.

The math on a 600-square-foot driveway with widespread damage: epoxy injection on 80 linear feet of crack ($800) + overlay on 200 sq ft of spalling ($1,400) + polyjacking on 150 sq ft of settled slab ($1,500) = $3,700 in repair. Full replacement on the same driveway: $4,500-$7,500. The repair option doesn't reset the slab clock; full replacement gives 25-50 more years.

Common root causes of concrete failure

Repair pricing assumes the work addresses the visible damage. Long-term repair success requires addressing root cause, not just symptom.

Deicing salt damage

The leading cause of residential concrete spalling in freeze-thaw climates. The U.S. Environmental Protection Agency identifies sodium chloride as the most damaging common deicer for concrete. Calcium magnesium acetate is significantly less damaging but 5-8x more expensive. Sealing the concrete every 3-5 years is the most cost-effective prevention.

Base failure

Poor subgrade compaction at original install, or soil washout from a leaking pipe, downspout, or grading issue. Per the Federal Highway Administration, base preparation is the single largest determinant of long-term concrete performance — and it cannot be fixed from above the slab.

Low PSI mix at original pour

Concrete below 3,500 PSI is more permeable, less freeze-thaw resistant, and spalls earlier. Common on driveways from the 1960s-1980s when 3,000 PSI was the residential default. Repair of a low-PSI slab buys time; doesn't extend total life dramatically.

Shallow rebar

Per ACI standards, rebar in exterior concrete should have a minimum 1.5 inches of cover. Shallow rebar rusts, expands roughly 7x its original volume, and pops the surface concrete off. Repair requires either restoring proper cover or removing the rebar entirely.

Sealing as prevention

Sealing concrete every 3-5 years (more often in salt-belt climates) is the most cost-effective prevention against the failure modes that drive repair. Cost: $0.50-$1.50 per square foot. On a 600-square-foot driveway, that's $300-$900 every 3-5 years. Total cost over a 30-year service life: $1,800-$5,400 in sealing — versus a typical $3,000-$5,000+ in repair work the unsealed driveway will need. For the full sealing breakdown, see Cost of Concrete's concrete driveways service hub.

Frequently asked questions

How much does it cost to repair a crack in concrete in 2026?

Concrete crack repair runs $5-$15 per linear foot in 2026, with most residential cracks falling between $8-$12. Hairline cracks under 1/8 inch can be sealed with a brush-on or self-leveling sealer at the low end. Active or wider cracks (1/8 to 1/2 inch) require epoxy or polyurethane injection at the upper end. A typical 10-foot crack repair runs $80-$120; a full driveway with multiple cracks ranges $300-$900.

What does it cost to fix spalling on a concrete driveway?

Concrete spalling repair runs $4-$12 per square foot in 2026 depending on depth. Surface spalling (top 1/4 inch) is resurfaced with a 1/4 inch polymer-modified overlay at $4-$8 per square foot. Deeper spalling (1/2 to 1 inch) requires removal back to sound concrete plus a partial-depth patch at $8-$12 per square foot. According to the American Concrete Institute, spalling almost always traces back to one of three root causes: salt damage, low PSI mix, or shallow rebar — all of which determine repair vs full replace.

How much does mudjacking cost vs polyjacking?

Mudjacking runs $3-$8 per square foot of affected slab area; polyjacking runs $6-$12 per square foot. Polyjacking is roughly 50-100 percent more expensive upfront but is now the dominant method in most U.S. metros because polyurethane foam cures in 15 minutes vs 24-48 hours for cement slurry, weighs roughly 5 percent of what cement slurry weighs, and resists water saturation that can re-soften traditional mudjacking grout.

When is concrete repair not worth it compared to replacement?

Repair stops being cost-effective above roughly 30 percent slab damage by area, or when the underlying base has failed (rather than just the slab). On a 600-square-foot driveway, repairing $4,000-$5,000 worth of crack injection, spalling patch, and settlement leveling almost matches the cost of full replacement at $4,500-$7,500 — without resetting the lifespan clock. Repaired sections continue to age at their existing rate.

Can I do concrete crack repair myself?

Hairline crack sealing with brush-on or self-leveling polyurethane sealer is a DIY-feasible job at $25-$60 in material per 100 linear feet. Epoxy injection for structural cracks requires pressure injection equipment and proper crack preparation — DIY kits exist at $40-$120 but consumer-grade epoxy and improper injection technique account for most of the failure rate. According to industry data, professional epoxy injection has roughly a 90 percent first-attempt success rate vs 45-60 percent for DIY.

How much does it cost to replace a single slab section?

Partial slab replacement (one full slab section between control joints) runs $7-$15 per square foot in 2026 — same per-square-foot pricing as a full driveway replacement, but with a higher floor for small areas because of mobilization. A typical 8 x 10 ft slab section replacement runs $700-$1,400. The cost premium vs simple repair is justified when settlement, base failure, or extensive cracking is contained to one section.

How long do concrete repairs last?

Polyurethane crack injection lasts 15-25 years on properly prepared cracks. Surface resurfacing (overlay) lasts 8-15 years before requiring redo. Mudjacking lasts 8-15 years; polyjacking lasts 20-30+ years because polyurethane foam doesn't degrade or re-saturate. Partial slab section replacement lasts the same as new concrete (25-50 years) because it is new concrete. Repair lifespan is highly dependent on whether the root cause was addressed — a crack injected without fixing the underlying settlement will reopen.

What causes concrete to spall, crack, or settle?

Spalling: deicing salt damage, low PSI mix, shallow rebar (steel rusts and expands), or freeze-thaw cycles on un-air-entrained concrete. Cracking: shrinkage during cure (hairline), poor control joint placement, settlement of the base, frost heave, or overload. Settlement: poor subgrade compaction, soil washout from a leaking pipe or downspout, or expansive clay swelling and shrinking. The Federal Highway Administration cites base preparation as the single most important determinant of long-term concrete performance across all three failure modes.

Key takeaways

  • Concrete repair splits into four pricing categories: crack injection ($5-$15/lf), spalling ($4-$12/sf), settlement leveling ($3-$12/sf), partial section replacement ($7-$15/sf).
  • The 30 percent rule: repair below 30 percent slab damage by area, replace above.
  • Polyjacking has replaced mudjacking as the dominant settlement-leveling method in most metros — 50-100 percent more upfront, 2-3x longer lifespan.
  • Crack injection lifespan is highly dependent on whether the underlying cause (base failure, settlement) was addressed.
  • Sealing every 3-5 years at $300-$900 per service is the cheapest insurance against the failure modes that drive repair cost.
  • Most contractors have a $250-$500 minimum service call, so very small repairs are proportionally expensive.

Need a contractor whose repair quote matches these numbers? Cost of Concrete lists vetted contractors by city, sourced from real homeowner-reported pricing. Browse the national directory at best concrete contractors or open your city's page to compare local repair pricing line by line against the national ranges above.
